quotes.text: Ask not of (a person's) descent, but ask about his conduct.
THE young man Vasettha said: “When one is virtuous and full of (good) works, in this way he becomes a Brāhmaṇa.”
NOT by birth does one become low caste, not by birth a Brâhmaṇa; by his deeds he becomes low caste, by his deeds he becomes a Brâhmaṇa.
THAT man who ... speaks falsely when asked as a witness, let us know such as a 'base-born.'
WHOSOEVER takes dishonestly the property of others, ... or (repudiates his lawful debts), let us know such as a 'base-born.'
WHOSOEVER ... harms living beings, ... and in whom there is no compassion for them, let us know such as a 'base-born.'
IN whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brâhmaṇa.
WHOSE hurts not (living) creatures, whether those that tremble or those that are strong, nor yet kills nor causes to be killed, him do I call a Brâhmaṇa.
WHOSE is (entirely) divested of sin, as is the heaven of mire and the moon of dust, him do I call a Brâhamana.
HIM I call indeed a Brâhmaṇa who, though he be guilty of no offence, patiently endures reproaches, bonds, and stripes.
WE will ... patiently suffer ... threats and blows ... at the hands of foolish men.
WHO, though he be cursed by the world, yet cherishes no ill-will towards it.
PERSECUTIONS and revilings, murders and numberless imprisonments, these hast thou suffered in thousands from the world, verily delighting in long-suffering.
quotes.note: See also Vâseṭṭha-sutta (v. 30), and Udâna varga (ch. 33, v. 20).

quotes.text: AT the end of life the soul goes forth alone; whereupon only our good deeds befriend us.
THE wrongdoer, devoid of rectitude, ... is full of anxiety when death arrives.
HE who has done what is right is free from fear.
WHATSOEVER a man has done, whether virtuous or sinful deeds, not one of them is of little importance; they all bear some kind of fruit.
OUR deeds, whether good or evil, ... follow us as shadows.
HE who now gives in charity Shall surely reap where he has given; For whosoever piously bestows a little water, Shall receive return like the great ocean.
COVETOUS desire is the greatest (source of) sorrow. Appearing as a friend, in secret 'tis our enemy.
THAT which is given in charity is rich in returns; therefore charity is a true friend; although it scatters it brings no remorse.
HE who ... stints the profit he has made, his wealth will soon be spent and lost.

THE (real) treasure is that laid up ... through charity and piety, temperance and self-control. ...The treasure thus hid is secure, and passes not away. Though he leave the fleeting riches of the world, this a man carries whith him — a treasure that no wrong of others, and no thief, can steal.
THOUGH exalted, forget not the lowly.
BE kind to all that lives.
NOT hurting any creature.
EXALTED by his virtues, but lowly through modesty.
OF all possessions, contentedness is the best by far.
A CONTENTED mind is always joyful.
LET us then live happily, though we call nothing our own.
NOT the whole world, ... the ocean-girt earth, With all the seas and the hills that girdle it, Would I wish to possess with shame added thereto.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 5, sec. 17)

quotes.text: LET none be forgetful of his own duty for the sake of another’s.
THE faults of others are easily seen: ... one’s own faults are difficult to see.
SELF-EXAMINATION is painful.
A MAN winnows his neighbour’s faults like chaff: his own he hides, as a cheat the bad die from the gambler.
